gh-w4-f1.answer:
Inheritance — reuse code by extending a parent class. IS-A relationship: Car IS-A Vehicle.

Requirements:
1. Create Vehicle.java (parent/superclass): fields brand, year; constructor; void start() prints "Vehicle starting"; void displayInfo().
2. Create Car.java extends Vehicle: add field int doors; constructor calling super(brand, year); override displayInfo() to include doors.
3. Create Bike.java extends Vehicle: add int engineCC; override start() to print "Bike kick-start".
4. Create InheritanceExamples.java with main:
   • Create Car and Bike objects.
   • Call start(), displayInfo() on each.
   • Show super keyword in child constructors.
5. Demonstrate: child inherits parent methods; override changes behavior.

Key concepts:
• extends — child gets all non-private fields and methods from parent.
• super(...) — calls parent constructor; must be first line in child constructor.
• Method overriding — same method signature in child replaces parent version.
• @Override annotation (optional) documents intent.
• IS-A: Car is a Vehicle — use inheritance only when this relationship is true.
gh-w4-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Framework classes extend base types everywhere — HttpServlet extends GenericServlet, ArrayList extends AbstractList. Inheritance enables polymorphism and code reuse across millions of Java libraries.

Compile & run:
javac Vehicle.java Car.java Bike.java InheritanceExamples.java
java InheritanceExamples

Push to java-week-4-advanced-oop:
git add Vehicle.java Car.java Bike.java InheritanceExamples.java
git commit -m "Add InheritanceExamples — extends and super"
git push

Rule: Java supports single inheritance — one extends per class.

gh-w4-f2.answer:
Polymorphism — one reference type, many forms. Runtime decides which method runs.

Requirements:
1. Reuse Vehicle, Car, Bike from inheritance (or define Shape/Circle/Rectangle alternative).
2. Create PolymorphismExamples.java with main demonstrating:
   • Parent reference, child object: Vehicle v = new Car(...);
   • v.start() — if overridden in Car, Car's version runs (dynamic dispatch).
   • Method parameter polymorphism: void testVehicle(Vehicle v) { v.start(); v.displayInfo(); }
   • Call testVehicle with Car and Bike objects.
   • Array of parent type: Vehicle[] fleet = { new Car(...), new Bike(...), new Car(...) }; loop and call start() on each.
3. Optional: instanceof check before casting — if (v instanceof Car) { Car c = (Car) v; }

Key concepts:
• Compile-time type vs runtime type — Vehicle ref can point to Car object.
• Dynamic method dispatch — JVM calls the actual object's overridden method.
• Upcasting is automatic: Car c = new Car(); Vehicle v = c;
• Downcasting requires explicit (Car) v and instanceof guard.
• Polymorphism enables extensibility — add new Vehicle types without changing testVehicle().
gh-w4-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: Spring dependency injection, GUI event handlers, and plugin architectures rely on polymorphism — code works with interfaces/superclasses while concrete implementations vary.

Compile & run:
javac *.java
java PolymorphismExamples

Push to java-week-4-advanced-oop:
git add PolymorphismExamples.java
git commit -m "Add PolymorphismExamples — runtime dispatch"
git push

gh-w4-f3.answer:
Abstract classes — define a template with abstract methods that subclasses must implement.

Requirements:
1. Create abstract class Shape:
   • Field String color
   • Constructor Shape(String color)
   • abstract double calculateArea();
   • abstract double calculatePerimeter();
   • concrete void displayColor() { print color }
2. Create Circle extends Shape: field double radius; implement both abstract methods (area = PI*r*r, perimeter = 2*PI*r).
3. Create Rectangle extends Shape: fields double length, width; implement area and perimeter.
4. AbstractClassDemo.java main: create Circle and Rectangle, print area/perimeter for each.
5. Try Shape s = new Shape() — should NOT compile; explain why.

Key concepts:
• abstract class — cannot be instantiated directly.
• abstract method — no body, ends with semicolon; subclass MUST override.
• Abstract class can have both abstract and concrete methods.
• Use abstract class when subclasses share common code but differ in specifics.
• vs Interface: abstract class can have state and constructors; choose based on design (Week 4 interface demo next).
gh-w4-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: Framework base classes (HttpServlet, AbstractList, JFrame) are abstract — you subclass and fill in hooks. Template Method pattern in enterprise Java builds on abstract classes.

Compile & run:
javac Shape.java Circle.java Rectangle.java AbstractClassDemo.java
java AbstractClassDemo

Push to java-week-4-advanced-oop:
git add Shape.java Circle.java Rectangle.java AbstractClassDemo.java
git commit -m "Add AbstractClassDemo — abstract class and subclasses"
git push

gh-w4-f4.answer:
Interfaces — define contracts (what a class can do) without implementation. Java supports multiple interfaces per class.

Requirements:
1. Create interface Drawable { void draw(); }
2. Create interface Resizable { void resize(double factor); }
3. Create class GraphicCircle implements Drawable, Resizable:
   • fields: double radius, String color
   • draw() prints circle details
   • resize(f) multiplies radius by factor
4. Create class GraphicRectangle implements Drawable only.
5. InterfaceDemo.java main:
   • Use interface references: Drawable d = new GraphicCircle(...); d.draw();
   • Array Drawable[] shapes — loop and call draw() (polymorphism via interface).
   • Show class implements multiple interfaces.

Key concepts:
• interface Name { method signatures; } — methods public abstract by default.
• implements Interface1, Interface2 — class promises to provide all methods.
• Interface reference can hold any implementing object.
• Interface vs abstract class: multiple implements OK; interface defines capability (Drawable, Comparable, Runnable).
• Java 8+: interfaces can have default and static methods — not required this week.
gh-w4-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Java APIs are interface-driven — List, Map, Runnable, Comparable. Spring uses interfaces for dependency injection. Testing uses mock implementations of interfaces.

Compile & run:
javac Drawable.java GraphicCircle.java InterfaceDemo.java
java InterfaceDemo

Push to java-week-4-advanced-oop:
git add Drawable.java Resizable.java GraphicCircle.java InterfaceDemo.java
git commit -m "Add InterfaceDemo — implements and contracts"
git push

gh-w4-f5.answer:
Exception handling — graceful recovery when things go wrong instead of crashing.

Requirements:
1. Create ExceptionHandlingDemo.java demonstrating:
   • try-catch: int result = 10 / 0; catch ArithmeticException, print friendly message.
   • ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: access invalid index, catch and explain.
   • NumberFormatException: Integer.parseInt("abc"), catch and prompt valid input.
   • Multiple catch blocks or catch (Exception e) as fallback.
   • finally block: print "Cleanup done" — runs whether exception occurred or not.
   • try-with-resources (optional): Scanner in try() auto-closes.
2. Custom scenario: method withdraw(double amount) throws IllegalArgumentException if amount < 0 — call in try-catch.
3. Print e.getMessage() in catch blocks for debugging.

Key concepts:
• try { risky code } catch (SpecificException e) { handle } finally { always runs }
• Exception = recoverable problem; Error = serious JVM issue (don't catch Error).
• throw new IllegalArgumentException("message") — create and throw manually.
• throws in method signature — caller must handle (declare or try-catch).
• Never use empty catch { } — always log or handle.
gh-w4-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Production apps catch network failures, invalid user input, and file-not-found gracefully. Uncaught exceptions become 500 errors — exception handling is mandatory in enterprise Java.

Compile & run:
javac ExceptionHandlingDemo.java
java ExceptionHandlingDemo

Observe: program continues after caught exceptions instead of terminating.

Push to java-week-4-advanced-oop:
git add ExceptionHandlingDemo.java
git commit -m "Add ExceptionHandlingDemo — try-catch-finally"
git push

gh-w4-f6.answer:
File handling — read and write text files using java.io.

Requirements:
1. Create FileHandlingDemo.java demonstrating:
   • Write to file: FileWriter or PrintWriter — write 3–4 lines to notes.txt.
   • Read from file: Scanner or BufferedReader on new File("notes.txt").
   • Print file contents line by line to console.
   • Append mode: new FileWriter("notes.txt", true) to add without overwriting.
2. Wrap file operations in try-catch for FileNotFoundException and IOException.
3. Optional try-with-resources:
   try (PrintWriter pw = new PrintWriter("notes.txt")) { pw.println("Hello"); }
4. Student record export (bonus): write Student data as CSV line to students.txt.
5. Confirm file exists in project folder after run.

Key concepts:
• FileWriter/PrintWriter — write text; set true for append.
• Scanner/BufferedReader — read text line by line.
• Always close streams (or use try-with-resources).
• Relative path notes.txt creates file in current working directory.
• IOException — checked exception; must handle or declare throws.
gh-w4-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Logs, config files, CSV imports, report exports — file I/O is everywhere before databases. Spring Boot reads application.properties via file handling under the hood.

Compile & run:
javac FileHandlingDemo.java
java FileHandlingDemo

Check notes.txt appeared in folder with expected content.

Push to java-week-4-advanced-oop:
git add FileHandlingDemo.java
git commit -m "Add FileHandlingDemo — read and write files"
git push

Add notes.txt to .gitignore if it contains test data you don't want committed — or commit a sample file intentionally.

gh-w4-f7.answer:
Library Management System — capstone combining OOP, inheritance (optional), arrays, file I/O, and exceptions.

Requirements:
1. Enhance Book class: title, author, isbn, isAvailable; optionally Member class (id, name).
2. LibraryManagementSystem with Book[] catalog, menu:
   1. Add Book
   2. Display All Books
   3. Search (title or author)
   4. Issue Book (by isbn, mark unavailable)
   5. Return Book
   6. Save to File (write all books to library.txt)
   7. Load from File (read back on startup — optional)
   8. Exit
3. Use exception handling for invalid input and file errors.
4. Validate: cannot issue unavailable book.
5. Organize across multiple files: Book.java, LibraryManagementSystem.java.

Key concepts:
• Capstone = many Week 2–4 skills in one project.
• Persist data to file so it survives program restart.
• Layered design: Book (entity), LibraryManagementSystem (UI + logic).
• Search with String.contains() or equalsIgnoreCase().
gh-w4-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: Library systems map to real ISO software — catalog, checkout, returns, persistence. This is a strong GitHub showcase project for internship applications.

Compile & run:
javac Book.java LibraryManagementSystem.java
java LibraryManagementSystem

Add books, issue/return, save file, verify library.txt.

gh-w4-f8.answer:
Bank Management System — manage multiple BankAccount objects with transactions and validation.

Requirements:
1. Reuse/enhance BankAccount from Week 3 (private fields, deposit, withdraw, getBalance).
2. BankManagementSystem.java:
   1. Create Account (account number, name, opening balance)
   2. Deposit to Account
   3. Withdraw from Account
   4. Transfer between Accounts
   5. Display All Accounts
   6. Search by Account Number
   7. Exit
3. Store BankAccount[] array; track count.
4. Transfer: withdraw from source, deposit to destination; rollback message if insufficient funds.
5. Use exceptions for invalid amounts (IllegalArgumentException).
6. Optional: write transaction log to transactions.txt using FileHandling patterns.

Key concepts:
• Transfer = two coordinated operations on two objects.
• Find account by number before operating — helper method findAccount(String num).
• Encapsulation ensures balance only changes via deposit/withdraw.
• Menu + array + entity class = standard enterprise console architecture.
gh-w4-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Core banking modules — accounts, transfers, transaction logs — mirror this design. Understanding transfer integrity (debit + credit) is fundamental to fintech interviews.

Compile & run:
javac BankAccount.java BankManagementSystem.java
java BankManagementSystem

Test transfer: Account A 10000 → transfer 3000 to B → verify balances.

Push to java-week-4-advanced-oop:
git add BankAccount.java BankManagementSystem.java
git commit -m "Add BankManagementSystem — accounts and transfers"
git push

gh-w4-f9.answer:
Hospital Management System — model Patients, Doctors, and Appointments using OOP.

Requirements:
1. Patient.java: id, name, age, ailment; display().
2. Doctor.java: id, name, specialization; display().
3. Appointment.java: fields patientId, doctorId, date, time; display().
4. HospitalManagementSystem.java with arrays for patients, doctors, appointments.
5. Menu:
   1. Register Patient
   2. Register Doctor
   3. Book Appointment (link patient + doctor + datetime)
   4. Display All Patients
   5. Display All Doctors
   6. Display All Appointments
   7. Exit
6. Validate: patient and doctor must exist before booking.
7. Optional: search patient by id; count appointments per doctor.

Key concepts:
• Multi-entity system — relationships via IDs (like foreign keys in databases).
• Appointment links two entities — precursor to relational data modeling.
• Separate class per entity; manager class coordinates.
• Real-world complexity with clear class boundaries.
gh-w4-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: Hospital ERP, clinic booking apps (Practo), and healthcare APIs use Patient/Doctor/Appointment models. This project demonstrates multi-entity OOP — highly relevant for healthcare IT roles.

Compile & run:
javac Patient.java Doctor.java Appointment.java HospitalManagementSystem.java
java HospitalManagementSystem

Register 2 patients, 2 doctors, book 2 appointments, display all.

gh-w4-f10.code:
# java-week-4-advanced-oop

Week 4 Java — Advanced OOP and capstone projects for Prathyu Academy.

## Topics covered
- Inheritance (extends, super, overriding)
- Polymorphism (dynamic dispatch, instanceof)
- Abstract classes and abstract methods
- Interfaces (implements, multiple interfaces)
- Exception handling (try-catch-finally, throw)
- File I/O (read/write text files)
- Capstone: Library, Bank, and Hospital management systems

## How to run
Requires JDK 17+. Complete Week 3 first.

```bash
javac *.java
java InheritanceExamples
java LibraryManagementSystem
java BankManagementSystem
java HospitalManagementSystem
```

## Concept map
| File | Concepts |
|------|----------|
| InheritanceExamples.java | extends, super, override |
| PolymorphismExamples.java | parent ref, runtime dispatch |
| AbstractClassDemo.java | abstract class/methods |
| InterfaceDemo.java | implements, contracts |
| ExceptionHandlingDemo.java | try-catch-finally |
| FileHandlingDemo.java | FileReader/FileWriter |
| LibraryManagementSystem.java | CRUD + file persistence |
| BankManagementSystem.java | transfers, validation |
| HospitalManagementSystem.java | multi-entity relationships |

## What I learned
Week 4 taught me how to reuse code with inheritance, define contracts with interfaces, handle errors gracefully, and persist data to files. The three capstone projects showed me how real applications combine many classes into one system.
